claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you check your eligibility for Child Benefit! Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child (or children) you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know the age(s) of the child(ren) you're asking about, and their name(s) if you're comfortable sharing.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm asking about my son Alex - he's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Lovely to meet you! Thanks for that information about Alex. Since Alex is 8 years old, he falls into the **under 16** category, which is great news as children under 16 automatically qualify in terms of age.

Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Alex:**

Is Alex working **less than 24 hours per week** in paid employment (or not working at all)?

At 8 years old, I expect he's not in paid work, but I just need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not working at all - he's only 8, so he's in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes complete sense - Alex is in primary school and not working at all, so that's a clear "yes" to working less than 24 hours per week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Is Alex **not** receiving any of the following benefits in his own right?
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

These are benefits that some older children/young people might claim themselves. Given Alex is 8, I expect this isn't applicable, but I just need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just a child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming. That's exactly what I'd expect for an 8-year-old. Let's move on to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Is Alex currently doing an apprenticeship?

Given he's 8 and in primary school, I'm confident the answer is no, but I just need to confirm he's **not** in any kind of apprenticeship programme.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - he's only 8 and in primary school. He's not doing any apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely! Just ticking the boxes here. Let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **one** of these to qualify:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You're a resident in the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can support yourself financially (without claiming certain benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as a family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Do any of these apply to you? For most people, the first option (normally living in the UK) is the most common.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you! Living in Manchester means you meet the residency requirement as you normally live in the UK. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about your responsibility for Alex:**

Are you responsible for Alex? You meet this requirement if **any** of the following apply:

1. **You live with Alex** - He lives in your home with you
2. **You contribute to his upkeep** - You pay at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards his care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** - You make maintenance payments for Alex under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that's straightforward - Alex lives with you, so you clearly meet the responsibility requirement. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

- **No one else is claiming** - You would be the only person claiming for Alex
- **Someone else is claiming but they agree to transfer to you** - Another person (e.g., the other parent) is claiming but has agreed to let you claim instead
- **There's a dispute** - Another person is claiming and you don't agree on who should receive it

Which situation applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is claiming for Alex. It's just me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that keeps things simple. Let's move on to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about Alex's living situation:**

Where is Alex currently living? Is he:

- **In hospital** - Currently staying in hospital
- **In care** - Currently in local authority care
- **Being fostered** - Currently in a foster care arrangement
- **Living normally at home** - Living at home with you in a normal family situation

Which of these describes Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in hospital for a while now, actually. He's been there for about 3 months.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear that Alex has been in hospital. I hope he's getting the care he needs and recovers well.

Let me check the rules around hospital stays.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I understand Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months (roughly 12-13 weeks).

Here's what you need to know about the rules for children in hospital:

- **Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks** while a child is in hospital
- After 12 weeks, eligibility depends on whether you're **regularly spending money on Alex's needs**

**Important cumulative rule:** If Alex has had multiple hospital stays with less than 28 days between them, those stays are added together towards the 12-week limit.

Since Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months, he may be at or just over the 12-week threshold. 

**Next question:**

Are you **regularly spending money on Alex's needs** while he's in hospital? This could include things like:
- Bringing him clothes, toys, or personal items
- Buying him food, snacks, or treats
- Paying for entertainment (books, games, etc.)
- Travel costs to visit him
- Any other expenses for his care and comfort

Are you contributing to his needs in this way?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I still buy his clothes, magazines, and his favorite food to bring to him when I visit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's lovely that you're supporting Alex in that way - it clearly shows you're still contributing to his needs while he's in hospital.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Since you're regularly spending money on Alex's needs (clothes, magazines, food, etc.), you meet the exception to the 12-week rule. This means you can continue to receive Child Benefit even though he's been in hospital for more than 12 weeks.

Let's continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question:**

Is the **local council NOT paying** towards Alex's hospital accommodation or maintenance?

In other words, is his hospital stay being funded through the NHS rather than through local council payments for his care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - it's just the NHS hospital care. The council isn't paying anything towards it.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful news! That's the final check complete. Let me now provide you with the outcome of your Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit for Alex!

Based on our assessment, you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit.

### Summary for Alex:
✅ **Age:** Under 16 (8 years old)  
✅ **Residency:** You live in the UK (Manchester)  
✅ **Responsibility:** Alex lives with you  
✅ **No other claimants:** You're the only person claiming  
✅ **Hospital stay:** Although Alex has been in hospital for over 12 weeks, you qualify for the exception because you're regularly spending money on his needs (clothes, magazines, food)  
✅ **Council not paying:** His care is through the NHS, not the local council  

---

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2 at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)
2. **Documents you'll need:**
   - Proof of your identity
   - Alex's birth certificate
3. **Payments:** Usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### Important Note:
If you or your partner earns over **£60,000** per year, you may be affected by the **High Income Child Benefit Charge**. You can still claim, but some or all of the benefit may need to be repaid through tax. You can find more details on the gov.uk website.

---

I hope Alex recovers soon. Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), so he qualifies based on age. He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) and meets the residency requirement. The claimant lives with Alex and is therefore responsible for him. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months (over the 12-week limit), the claimant qualifies for the exception because they are regularly spending money on Alex's needs (clothes, magazines, food). The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation - it's NHS hospital care. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for longer than 12 weeks, the claimant meets the exception to the hospital rule by regularly contributing to Alex's needs during his stay. All other standard eligibility criteria are also met.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), so he qualifies based on age. He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) and meets the residency requirement. The claimant lives with Alex and is therefore responsible for him. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months (over the 12-week limit), the claimant qualifies for the exception because they are regularly spending money on Alex's needs (clothes, magazines, food). The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation - it's NHS hospital care. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for longer than 12 weeks, the claimant meets the exception to the hospital rule by regularly contributing to Alex's needs during his stay. All other standard eligibility criteria are also met.
